cards.title: Why Measure Delivery Performance
cards.teaser: Intuition about whether your team ships fast, recovers quickly, and breaks production rarely is almost always wrong. Measurement replaces opinion with signal — and the DORA research program identified exactly which signals predict elite delivery performance.

runs.text: Before 2014, most engineering organizations measured delivery performance with proxies that felt intuitive but predicted nothing useful: lines of code shipped, number of features in a release, velocity points completed. These metrics have in common that they are easy to collect and easy to game, and they correlate poorly with the outcomes organizations actually care about: reliability, customer satisfaction, and competitive speed.

The DORA research program, founded by Dr. Nicole Forsgren, Jez Humble, and Gene Kim, applied rigorous social-science methodology — surveys, structural equation modeling, and four years of longitudinal data — to answer a precise question: what measurable properties of software delivery predict organizational performance? The result was published as
Accelerate: The Science of Lean Software and DevOps
(2018) and refined in the annual State of DevOps reports. It is the most cited empirical study in the software delivery field.
The DORA findings established two things that reshaped how teams think about measurement:

items.text: Speed and stability are not a trade-off.
Elite performers deploy frequently
and
have lower failure rates than low performers. The assumption that shipping faster means breaking more is empirically false.
Four metrics predict everything else.

Deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery (MTTR) are the four outcome metrics that distinguish elite delivery organizations from low performers. All four are directly observable from pipeline and incident data.

runs.text: Understanding these metrics matters because pipelines are where all four of them are generated or damaged. A slow pipeline increases lead time. A pipeline that lacks proper testing inflates change failure rate. A pipeline without deployment automation extends MTTR. Measurement makes the connection between pipeline design decisions and delivery outcomes legible.

The DORA research covers 33,000+ respondents across six years and validates that high-performing software delivery organizations are 2x more likely to exceed profitability, market share, and productivity goals compared to low performers. Delivery performance is a business outcome, not just an engineering metric.

We measure delivery performance because intuition lies. The DORA research identified four specific metrics — from real pipeline and incident data — that separate teams that ship reliably from teams that struggle. Those four metrics are the subject of this chapter.

cards.title: The DORA Metrics Framework
cards.teaser: DORA's four metrics are organized across two dimensions: throughput (how fast you deliver) and stability (how reliably you deliver). Understanding the two-dimensional model prevents the most common measurement mistake — optimizing one dimension at the expense of the other.

runs.text: The four DORA metrics are not an arbitrary list — they are organized along two orthogonal dimensions that capture the full scope of software delivery health:

items.text: Throughput metrics
answer: how fast does the team deliver value? These are
deployment frequency
(how often you deploy to production) and
lead time for changes
(how long from commit to production). Together they measure the speed of the delivery system.
Stability metrics
answer: how reliably does the team deliver quality? These are
change failure rate
(what fraction of deployments cause production incidents) and
mean time to recovery
(how long to restore service after a failure). Together they measure the resilience of the delivery system.

runs.text: The two-dimensional structure is crucial. A team that deploys 50 times per day but causes a production incident on 40% of those deployments has excellent throughput and catastrophic stability. A team that ships once per quarter with a 0% failure rate has excellent stability and unacceptably low throughput. DORA research shows that elite performers achieve high scores on
both
dimensions simultaneously — they are not in tension.
The framework also implies a measurement strategy. To assess your delivery system, you need data from two sources:

items.text: Pipeline metadata
— deployment events, build durations, commit timestamps, and job exit codes. This data lives in your CI/CD platform (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins).
Incident data
— production incidents, their root causes, their start times, and their resolution times. This data lives in incident management platforms (PagerDuty, OpsGenie, Incident.io).

runs.text: In 2021, DORA added a fifth metric: Reliability (the ability to meet SLO targets). The original four remain the core framework. Reliability is best tracked via error budgets in an SRE practice and is complementary to, not a replacement for, the original four.

DORA gives you two throughput metrics (deploy often, deploy fast) and two stability metrics (break rarely, recover quickly). You need all four — a high score on just one side produces the wrong kind of engineering culture.

cards.title: Deployment Frequency
cards.teaser: Deployment frequency measures how often your team successfully deploys to production. It is the most direct measure of your delivery cadence — and the DORA research shows it is the single metric most predictive of organizational performance.

runs.text: Deployment frequency (DF) answers: how often does working software reach production users? A deployment, in DORA's definition, is any release of code to the primary production environment (or the primary production-like environment for teams without direct prod access). Hotfixes count. Automated rollouts count. Manual deploys of pre-built artifacts count.

The DORA research found that deployment frequency is remarkably bimodal in industry. Elite performers deploy to production on demand — multiple times per day. Low performers deploy once per month or less. The gap is not incremental: elite performers deploy 973x more frequently than low performers, according to the 2023 State of DevOps report.
What drives deployment frequency? The most important factors are:

items.text: Batch size.

Teams that deploy large batches of changes deploy infrequently — each release accumulates risk and requires a release ceremony. Teams that deploy small changes (individual features, single bug fixes) deploy constantly because each change is low-risk and self-contained.
Pipeline automation.
Manual deployment steps — approvals, checklists, handoffs — create friction that reduces frequency. Fully automated deployment pipelines remove the friction.
Feature flags.
Decoupling deployment from release (shipping code that is dark behind a flag) allows teams to deploy continuously while controlling when users see changes.
Trunk-based development.
Long-lived feature branches reduce frequency by forcing batch merges. Trunk-based development with short-lived branches or direct commits enables continuous deployment.

runs.text: Measuring deployment frequency from GitHub Actions is straightforward: query workflow runs filtered to your deployment workflow and the production environment, count successful runs per time window (day, week, month). A GitHub Actions query using the REST API:

items.text: GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs?event=push&status=success&branch=main
— filter to runs on the production branch with a successful status. Count these over a rolling 30-day window to get monthly deployment frequency.

runs.text: Do not count pipeline runs as deployments unless the pipeline actually deploys to production. A CI pipeline that builds and tests on every commit is not a deployment event. Only deployments to the primary production environment count toward deployment frequency.

Deployment frequency is simply: how many times per day, week, or month does your team successfully push changes to production? The higher the number, the smaller the batches, the lower the risk per change.

cards.title: Lead Time for Changes
cards.teaser: Lead time for changes measures the elapsed time from the first commit of a change to its arrival in production. It captures the full pipeline journey — and every minute of unnecessary waiting is visible in this number.

runs.text: Lead time for changes (LTC) is the elapsed wall-clock time from the moment code is committed to the moment that code is running in production and serving users. In DORA's precise definition: the time from "a commit is made to the trunk" to "that commit is in production."
Lead time aggregates the latency of every step in the delivery system:

items.text: Pipeline queue time
— how long the commit waits for a runner to become available.
Build duration
— compile, package, container image build.
Test duration
— unit tests, integration tests, contract tests.
Security scan duration
— SAST, dependency vulnerability scan, secrets detection.
Approval wait time
— if a human must approve before production deploy, the time that approval takes is part of lead time.
Deployment duration
— rolling out the artifact, running health checks, stabilizing.

runs.text: Elite performers achieve lead times under one hour. High performers measure lead times in days. Low performers measure lead times in weeks or months. The difference is not team size or product complexity — it is the degree to which the delivery pipeline eliminates waiting.
Measuring lead time from GitHub Actions requires correlating commit timestamps with deployment event timestamps. A common approach:

items.text: Record the commit SHA and timestamp when the pipeline starts.
Record the deployment completion timestamp when the production deploy job succeeds.
Lead time = deployment completion timestamp − commit timestamp.
Average or median across all deployments in the measurement window.

runs.text: Use median rather than mean for lead time. A single delayed release (waiting for a holiday approval window) will inflate the mean significantly; the median is more representative of typical experience.

Lead time is the metric most directly in the control of the engineering team. Deployment frequency depends partly on product decisions (release cadence, feature flags). Lead time depends almost entirely on how the pipeline is designed, parallelized, and optimized. A slow pipeline is always a lead time problem waiting to be measured.

Lead time for changes is the stopwatch from "developer presses commit" to "users get the change." Every stage of your pipeline adds to that number. Elite teams get it under an hour. The way to shrink it is to find and eliminate the waiting.

cards.title: Change Failure Rate
cards.teaser: Change failure rate measures what fraction of production deployments cause a degradation that requires a hotfix, rollback, or incident response. It is the DORA stability metric most directly influenced by pipeline test coverage.

runs.text: Change failure rate (CFR) is defined as: the percentage of deployments to production that result in a service degradation requiring remediation. Remediation includes hotfixes, rollbacks, patches, and forward-fixes deployed as emergency releases. A deployment that reaches production and works correctly has a CFR contribution of zero. A deployment that causes a P1 incident contributes one failed deployment to the numerator.
The formula is straightforward:

items.text: CFR = (deployments causing incidents) / (total deployments) × 100%

runs.text: Elite performers maintain a CFR of 0–5%. Low performers see CFRs of 46–60%. The gap is not primarily explained by team skill — it is explained by the presence or absence of automated quality gates in the pipeline.
The pipeline mechanisms that most directly reduce CFR:

items.text: Comprehensive automated testing.
Unit, integration, contract, and end-to-end tests that catch behavioral regressions before they reach production.
Pre-production environments.
Staging environments that closely mirror production surface environment-specific failures before they affect users.
Progressive delivery.
Canary deployments and feature flags limit the blast radius of a bad change. Even if a failure escapes testing, it affects a small fraction of users before being detected and rolled back.
Idempotent deployments.

A deployment script that can be safely re-run without side effects (idempotency) allows fast recovery when a deploy partially fails — re-running brings the system to the desired state without manual intervention.

runs.text: Measuring CFR requires linking deployment events (from the pipeline) to incident events (from PagerDuty or OpsGenie). A deployment "caused" an incident if an incident was opened within a configurable time window (typically 24 hours) after the deployment completed, and the incident's root cause is tagged to a code change.

CFR is the metric most often distorted by measurement politics. If incidents are under-reported (because engineers close them quickly to avoid blame), the CFR denominator is artificially inflated and CFR looks lower than it is. Accurate CFR requires a blameless incident culture and consistent incident logging.

Change failure rate is: out of every 100 deployments, how many broke production? Elite teams break production fewer than 5 times out of 100. The main way to reduce it is to catch more failures in the pipeline before they ship.

cards.title: MTTR (Mean Time to Recovery)
cards.teaser: MTTR measures how long it takes to restore service after a production incident. It is the DORA metric most sensitive to deployment automation: teams that can roll back in minutes recover in minutes; teams that require manual intervention recover in hours.

runs.text: Mean time to recovery (MTTR) measures the average elapsed time from the moment a production incident begins (service degradation starts) to the moment service is fully restored. It is sometimes called mean time to restore or mean time to repair — DORA uses "recover" to emphasize that the goal is restored user experience, not just a deployed fix.
MTTR has three components that each represent a stage of incident response:

items.text: Time to detect.
How long until the team knows the incident is happening. Determined by monitoring and alerting quality — how quickly metrics, logs, and traces surface the anomaly.
Time to diagnose.
How long until the team identifies the root cause. Determined by observability tooling and the team's familiarity with the system.
Time to restore.

How long to apply the fix. This is where pipeline automation has the largest impact: a team that can trigger an automated rollback in 30 seconds versus a team that requires a manual re-deploy with approvals will have dramatically different restoration times.

runs.text: Elite performers have an MTTR under one hour. Low performers measure MTTR in days. PagerDuty's incident analytics surfaces MTTR directly from incident open/close timestamps, segmented by service, team, or severity. A query for mean MTTR over a 90-day window, filtered to P1 and P2 incidents, is the standard baseline measurement.
The pipeline mechanisms that most directly reduce MTTR:

items.text: Automated rollback.
If health checks fail after deployment, the pipeline automatically redeploys the previous artifact. No human decision required.
Fast deployment pipelines.
A hotfix that takes 45 minutes to deploy adds 45 minutes to MTTR even after the fix is written. Pipeline speed is MTTR-critical.
Runbook automation.
Common recovery actions (restart a service, flush a cache, redirect traffic) automated as pipeline-triggered jobs reduce the time-to-restore to seconds.

runs.text: MTTR and deployment frequency are inversely related in high-performing teams: the more frequently you deploy, the more practiced your recovery automation becomes, and the faster your MTTR. Teams that deploy rarely have untested rollback procedures and slow, unfamiliar restoration paths.

MTTR is the stopwatch from "production broke" to "production is fixed." The pipeline controls the "restore" part of that time: teams with automated rollback recover in minutes; teams with manual deploys recover in hours or days.

cards.title: Performance Tiers: Elite, High, Medium, Low
cards.teaser: DORA segments delivery teams into four performance tiers based on their metric values. The tiers are not arbitrary bands — they are empirically derived clusters that predict organizational outcomes. Knowing which tier you are in tells you what to fix first.

runs.text: From the State of DevOps survey data, DORA identifies four distinct clusters of performance. Each cluster has characteristic ranges for all four metrics, and each maps to different organizational outcomes (profitability, productivity, growth, employee satisfaction).

items.text: Elite.

Deployment frequency: multiple deploys per day. Lead time: under one hour. Change failure rate: 0–5%. MTTR: under one hour. Elite performers are 2x more likely to report exceeding organizational performance goals.
High.

Deployment frequency: between once per day and once per week. Lead time: between one day and one week. Change failure rate: 5–10%. MTTR: under one day. High performers show strong organizational outcomes — similar to elite, but with more coordination overhead in the pipeline.
Medium.

Deployment frequency: between once per week and once per month. Lead time: between one week and one month. Change failure rate: 10–15%. MTTR: between one day and one week. Medium performers represent the majority of the industry and have significant headroom for improvement on all four metrics.
Low.

Deployment frequency: less than once per month. Lead time: between one month and six months. Change failure rate: 46–60%. MTTR: more than one week. Low performers are caught in a reinforcing negative loop: infrequent deployments mean large batches, large batches mean high failure rates, high failure rates mean teams are afraid to deploy, which reduces frequency further.

runs.text: The tiers are not destiny. The DORA research documents teams that moved from low to high performance in twelve to eighteen months by focusing on specific pipeline and culture investments. The most common sequence:

items.text: Low → Medium:
Invest in automated testing and CI. Reduce batch size. Establish a green-main policy.
Medium → High:
Automate deployment pipeline end-to-end. Implement trunk-based development. Add automated rollback.
High → Elite:
Add feature flags for decoupled deployment. Optimize pipeline for sub-hour lead time. Invest in observability for sub-hour MTTR.

runs.text: The 2023 State of DevOps report found that 18% of surveyed teams qualify as elite performers, up from 7% in 2018. The proportion of low performers has shrunk from 31% to 17%. The entire industry is shifting, which raises the competitive bar for what "good" delivery looks like.

Elite teams deploy dozens of times a day and fix incidents in under an hour. Low performers deploy once a month and take a week to recover. The tier you're in tells you exactly where to focus your pipeline investment to move up.

cards.title: Measuring DORA from Pipeline Metadata
cards.teaser: All four DORA metrics can be derived from data your pipeline already generates — deployment events, build logs, commit timestamps, and job exit codes. The challenge is not data collection; it is correct attribution and consistent definitions.

runs.text: The practical challenge of DORA measurement is not philosophical — it is operational. Most teams have the raw data they need scattered across GitHub, their CI platform, and their incident management tool. The work is connecting those sources with consistent definitions.
A minimal DORA measurement stack uses three data sources:

items.text: Version control (GitHub/GitLab)
— provides commit timestamps, pull request open/merge times, and branch metadata. These are the starting points for lead time calculation.
CI/CD platform (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI)

— provides workflow run events, job durations, deployment environment metadata, and job exit codes (success/failure). This is the source for deployment frequency, pipeline duration as a proxy for lead time, and build failure data.
Incident management (PagerDuty, OpsGenie, Incident.io)
— provides incident start times, resolution times, severity, and root cause tags. This is the source for MTTR and, combined with deployment data, for change failure rate.

runs.text: A GitHub Actions pipeline can emit deployment events to a metrics collector using the GitHub Deployments API. When the production deploy job succeeds, the pipeline calls:

items.text: POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments
— creates a deployment record with the commit SHA, environment, and timestamp.
POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments/{id}/statuses
— updates the deployment record with a success/failure state.

runs.text: These events feed directly into DORA dashboards via tools like LinearB, Sleuth, or a custom data pipeline into BigQuery or Datadog. The Four Keys open-source project from Google DORA provides a reference implementation using Google Cloud services that ingests GitHub, GitLab, and PagerDuty events and computes all four metrics.

The most common measurement error is conflating CI build success with deployment. A build that passes all tests but is never deployed to production contributes to neither deployment frequency nor lead time. Only events that result in a production deployment count.

Your pipeline already generates all the data DORA needs — commit timestamps, deployment events, job results. The work is wiring three systems together (version control, CI platform, incident management) and agreeing on what counts as a deployment and what counts as a failure.

cards.title: Build Status Badges and Build Logs
cards.teaser: Build status badges and build logs are the most immediate observability artifacts your pipeline produces — they are the first place engineers look when something breaks, and the primary signal that determines whether a team trusts their pipeline.

runs.text: A build status badge is a small image embedded in a repository's README that displays the current state of the default branch pipeline: passing, failing, or unknown. In GitHub Actions, a badge is generated at:

items.text: https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/actions/workflows/{workflow}.yml/badge.svg
— returns an SVG image that reflects the last completed run of the named workflow on the default branch.

runs.text: A badge is more than decoration. It establishes a
build status contract

: the badge is green when the pipeline passes, red when it fails. Teams that display the badge publicly hold themselves accountable to keeping the main branch green. The badge makes the pipeline's health visible to everyone — contributors, reviewers, and consumers of the library.
Build logs are the full output of every step in every job of every pipeline run. They are the primary diagnostic tool for understanding why a build failed. A well-structured build log:

items.text: Groups output by step with timestamps.
GitHub Actions automatically folds step output and shows per-step duration. This makes it fast to identify which step failed and how long it took.
Surfaces the exit code explicitly.

An exit code of 0 means success; any non-zero value means failure. GitHub Actions shows the exit code for failed steps. A job that exits with code 137 (OOM kill) tells a very different story than one that exits with code 1 (assertion failure).
Includes environment context.
Logging the runner OS, dependency versions, and tool versions at the start of the log prevents a class of "works on my machine" investigations where the log provides no environment context.
Does not log secrets.
GitHub Actions automatically masks values of secrets in logs. Custom scripts that construct commands with embedded secret values must be designed carefully to avoid logging the constructed string.

runs.text: Build logs also contribute to DORA measurement. A pipeline with a high build log failure rate (many runs ending in non-zero exit codes before the deploy stage) indicates a pipeline timeout, a flaky test, or a dependency issue — all of which inflate lead time and erode team trust in the pipeline.

Pipeline timeouts are a frequently misconfigured failure mode. A job with no explicit timeout will run until the platform's global limit (6 hours on GitHub Actions). Setting explicit timeouts at the job level (e.g., timeout-minutes: 20) ensures that a hung test suite fails quickly rather than consuming hours of runner minutes before producing a non-zero exit code.

A build status badge tells the world whether your main branch is passing right now. A build log tells you exactly which step failed, why, and with what exit code. Together they are the first two tools every engineer uses when a pipeline goes red.

cards.title: The SPACE Framework
cards.teaser: The SPACE framework, published by Forsgren et al. in 2021, extends DORA's quantitative metrics with qualitative dimensions — satisfaction, performance, activity, communication, and efficiency — to give a complete picture of developer productivity that no single metric can capture.

runs.text: DORA metrics measure the
output

of the delivery system: how often software reaches production and how reliably. They do not measure whether developers are satisfied with their tools, whether the team is communicating well, or whether engineers feel productive. The SPACE framework, introduced by Nicole Forsgren, Margaret-Anne Storey, Chandra Maddila, Thomas Zimmermann, Brian Houck, and Jenna Butler in a 2021 ACM Queue paper, addresses this gap.
SPACE is an acronym for five dimensions of developer productivity:

items.text: Satisfaction and well-being.

How fulfilled are developers with their work, tools, and team? Measured via surveys. Developer satisfaction is a leading indicator of retention and, per the DORA research, correlates with delivery performance.
Performance.
The outcomes of the work: reliability, absence of defects, customer impact. This is where DORA's four metrics sit within the SPACE model.
Activity.

The volume of work completed: PRs merged, commits made, code reviews completed. Activity metrics are easy to measure but dangerous in isolation — a high PR count with low quality is worse than a lower PR count with high quality.
Communication and collaboration.
How well the team integrates knowledge and works across boundaries. Measured by PR review latency, knowledge silos ("bus factor"), and cross-team dependency resolution times.
Efficiency and flow.
The degree to which engineers can work without interruptions, context-switching, or waiting. Measured by flow state survey data, interruption frequency, and meeting load.

runs.text: The SPACE paper's most important contribution is its warning:
no single metric captures developer productivity

. Optimizing for any one SPACE dimension at the expense of others produces distortions. A team measured only on activity metrics will generate noise (low-value commits, trivial PRs) to look productive. A team measured only on satisfaction will trend toward comfortable work with no delivery pressure.

SPACE is most powerful when used alongside DORA, not instead of it. DORA metrics tell you how the delivery system performs. SPACE metrics tell you whether the people operating that system are healthy. Both are needed to sustain high performance over time.

SPACE adds the human dimensions that DORA misses: are developers happy, are they communicating, do they feel like they're in flow? DORA tells you what the pipeline produces; SPACE tells you whether the people running it are sustainable.

cards.title: Goal-Setting vs Vanity Dashboards
cards.teaser: Most engineering dashboards measure what is easy to count, not what predicts outcomes. A DORA dashboard that reports metrics without targets, trends, or owners is a vanity dashboard — it signals sophistication without driving behavior.

runs.text: The gap between a useful metrics program and a vanity metrics program is narrow but consequential. Both programs produce dashboards. Only one of them changes team behavior.
A vanity metrics dashboard has these properties:

items.text: No targets.

The dashboard shows current values but does not specify what "good" looks like. A deployment frequency of 3.2 per month is displayed; whether that is improving, degrading, or acceptable is not stated.
No trends.
The dashboard shows point-in-time values rather than rolling trends. A metric that improved last quarter and is now degrading looks identical to one that has been stable.
No owners.
The dashboard exists but nobody is responsible for acting when a metric degrades. "We have a DORA dashboard" becomes a statement about tooling, not about culture.
No retrospective integration.
The metrics are not reviewed in sprint retrospectives, quarterly planning, or engineering all-hands. They are visible but not discussed.

runs.text: A useful metrics program has the opposite properties. The Google DORA team recommends treating DORA metrics with OKR-style goal-setting:

items.text: Set tier-based targets.
If you are a medium performer, set a quarterly target to reach high-performer thresholds on the metric where you have the most headroom.
Track 90-day rolling trends.
Short-term variance is noise; a 90-day trend is signal. Review trends monthly.
Assign metric ownership.
One team or person is accountable for each metric. That person reviews the metric weekly and raises an alert if it degrades significantly.
Integrate into retrospectives.
At the end of each sprint, review DORA metrics alongside velocity. If lead time increased this sprint, ask why — and make a specific pipeline improvement before the next sprint.

runs.text: Avoid using DORA metrics for performance reviews or team comparisons. The DORA research explicitly warns against this: metrics that become targets become gaming targets. A team evaluated on deployment frequency will generate meaningless micro-deployments. Metrics should drive system improvement, not individual or team accountability.

A DORA dashboard without targets, trends, and owners is wallpaper. For metrics to drive change, they need to be owned by someone who reviews them weekly, compared against a target, and discussed when they degrade.

cards.title: The Observability Pipeline
cards.teaser: An observability pipeline collects, routes, and enriches telemetry data — logs, metrics, and traces — from every stage of the delivery system. It is the infrastructure that makes DORA measurement, incident detection, and MTTR reduction possible at scale.

runs.text: Measuring DORA metrics in a single-repository team with one CI platform and one incident tool is straightforward. Measuring them across dozens of repositories, multiple CI platforms, and heterogeneous incident tooling requires a dedicated observability pipeline — a system for collecting, normalizing, routing, and storing telemetry data from every source in the delivery system.
An observability pipeline for CI/CD metrics has four logical layers:

items.text: Collection.

Telemetry is emitted from sources: GitHub Actions workflow run events (via webhooks), deployment status events (via GitHub Deployments API), PagerDuty incident webhooks, and application-level metrics (via Prometheus or StatsD). Each source emits raw, schema-inconsistent data.
Normalization.

A pipeline processor (OpenTelemetry Collector, a Lambda function, or a purpose-built tool like Sleuth or LinearB) transforms raw events into a canonical schema: timestamp, source, environment, commit SHA, deployment status, incident severity.
Routing.

Normalized events are routed to storage and analysis destinations: a time-series database (InfluxDB, Prometheus), a data warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake), or a metrics platform (Datadog, Grafana). Routing decisions may be rule-based (all deployment events to BigQuery; all error events to Datadog).
Analysis and alerting.

DORA dashboards, SLO tracking, and anomaly detection queries run against the stored, normalized data. Alerts fire when metrics degrade beyond configured thresholds — for example, if deployment frequency drops below a 7-day moving average.

runs.text: The observability pipeline is itself a pipeline — it must be reliable, have defined SLOs, and be monitored. A telemetry pipeline that drops events under load silently corrupts DORA measurements. The system that measures your delivery system must be held to the same engineering standards as the delivery system itself.

The OpenTelemetry project provides the most widely adopted open standard for observability data collection. CI/CD platforms are adding OpenTelemetry support: GitLab CI can emit pipeline traces in OTLP format, and GitHub Actions plugins exist for exporting job metrics as OpenTelemetry spans.

The Google DORA team's open-source Four Keys project (github.com/dora-team/fourkeys) provides a full reference implementation of an observability pipeline for DORA metrics using Google Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Run, BigQuery, and Looker Studio. It ingests GitHub, GitLab, and PagerDuty events and computes all four metrics out of the box — a useful starting point before building a custom solution.

An observability pipeline is the plumbing that connects your CI platform, your version control, and your incident tool into one place where DORA metrics can actually be computed. Without it, you are manually correlating spreadsheets instead of reading a dashboard.
